steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage describes two things: (1) narrow- and intermediate-headed fish have increasing snout pressure fluctuations as vortex size grows, while wide-headed fish stay low; (2) the second team hypothesizes that higher pressure fluctuations help fish avoid obstructions. We need a finding that directly supports that link: high fluctuations = more avoidance (fewer bumps), low fluctuations = less avoidance (more bumps).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the devil catfish (*Bagarius bagarius*), which has a relatively wide head, bumped into more than half of the obstructions.: Reports a single wide-headed species (low fluctuations, many bumps) but gives no high-fluctuation comparison group. Without a contrast, this doesn't test the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice B — A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the intermediate-headed dusky smooth-hound (*Mustelus canis*) bumped into the obstructions just as often as the wide-headed *Synodontis macropunctata* did.: If both bump equally, fluctuation level makes no difference — this contradicts, not supports, the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice C — A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that the wide-headed *Synodontis macropunctata* bumped into obstructions more often than the intermediate-headed dusky smooth-hound (*Mustelus canis*) did.: Wide-headed = low fluctuations = more bumps; intermediate-headed = high fluctuations = fewer bumps. This is exactly the contrast the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice D — A study using obstructions that created large vortices in low-visibility conditions found that some specimens of the intermediate-headed rainbow trout (*Oncorhynchus mykiss*) bumped into the obstructions more often than other specimens of the same fish did.: Variation within a single species tells us nothing about whether fluctuation level drives avoidance. ✗
C is correct.
